comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my forty-fifth published book and the last of my books to come out of my former blog, The Archdruid Report. The last theme I developed in any detail there was the recognition that blind faith in progress had cut most people off from the single most useful source of working options available to us -- that is, the past. My novel Retrotopia had sketched out that theme in fictional form, my nonfiction work After Progress had delved into the bogus religion that had arisen around the myth of progress, but I wanted to say a few things about the practical side of using the past as a resource for the future. A flurry of posts, and then this book, were the results.

It sold very poorly, and there were reasons for that. The peak oil movement had collapsed completely by the time it saw print in 2017, and all the publicity and money had long since been redirected into a moronically oversimplified narrative about climate change that insisted that we were all going to fry like pork chops on a grill sometime very soon unless billions of dollars were flushed down the ratholes of photovoltaic solar energy and giant wind turbines, both of which had already been tested and found inadequate. All the other issues surrounding pollution and resource depletion, and every other aspect of the predicament of industrial society, got stuffed down the memory hole in a hurry in those years. The project of reviving older, less extravagant, and more resilient technologies from the past? Nobody was interested. A new edition will be out this October, and you can order a copy here; we'll see if the unraveling of the status quo under way right now gives it a chance to be heard.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Is Renaissance astrological magic generally compatible with Christianity? Do you know if Warnock’s planetary magic books are?

I’m thinking of That Hideous Strength, which depicts both Christianity and planetary magic working in harmony. What system would say comes closest to what is in Lewis’s book, if you know?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Most of the Renaissance mages who originally practiced that form of magic were Christians who went to Mass every Sunday, and some of them were priests. (Marsilio Ficino, for example, who was one of the greatest of Renaissance mages, was in holy orders.) Chris's books aren't specifically religious in focus but can be adapted to Christian practice very easily.

2) Lewis's discussions of magic in that book are based on Dion Fortune's magical writings and practice -- I have no idea what Lewis was doing with that material, but if you know Fortune's work the parallels are impossible to miss. Of course Fortune was also a Christian -- I recommend her book Mystical Meditations on the Collects as a guide to that side of her work.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
C.S. Lewis was friends with Charles Williams (another of the Inklings). Williams had connections with (or was a memebr of) Waite's Holy Order of the Golden Dawn.
A number of times I've seen questions here about good occult fiction. Charles Williams' books slipped my mind, but there's some great stuff there. My personal favorite is "War in Heaven" about a very mundane seeming English cleric who has ended up with the Holy Grail as one of the implements he uses for the Mass. This book even includes Prestor John! Williams was another writer like Lewis who truly understood the subtlety and horror of real spiritual evil. Anyway, Williams, Lewis, Tolkein, Waite, Golden Dawn... 'Nuff said...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Granted, but I know Williams's work in quite some detail -- also Waite's writings -- and that's not what Lewis used for his magic. It's very distinctly Dion Fortune, complete with the focus on what her school called the Arthurian Formula. That's the fascinating thing about Lewis's planetary trilogy...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Fair enough. My thinking was that Williams was a connection between the Inklings and the schisming world of the Golden Dawn, and that was a small world. Given the intellectual calibre and curiosity of each of these men, and Lewis's particular interest in both Christianity and the Arthurian mythology, it wouldn't be at all surprising if he was in touch with Fortune. Given his thought that there was a "righteous paganism" (can't remember where I saw that), but that Christianity was the perfection and completion of human religion, it wouldn't be surprising to find that he and Fortune didn't find some resonances with each other's work. Doubt he was a frequenter of Queensborough Terrace though...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Would a planetary talisman be considered a "planetary working?" I am on the brink of buying a planetary talisman from Warnocke. Is that safe without having done planetary workings?

replies.title: (no subject)

You might consider meditating on each of the planets and the planetary gods, just to help activate them all in your psyche, but either way you should be fine.

comments.title: Rootwork and Ceremonial Magic
comments.content:
I've been doing some background reading on Rootwork and Southern Conjure and noticed a class of workings where the practitioner uses something tied to the target as a guide to breath in the target's essence and then do something with it such as fix the essence on a doll or golem for further work. The description of the spell seems to suggest breathing in the essence from the actual target. I was wondering whether the CGD Middle Pillar with its wrapping curtains of light would block or otherwise prevent this process. It looks like a matter of asking an Astral spell to block an Etheric spell, but I know after doing rituals daily for awhile I can start feeling energy moving, so maybe the Astral Middle Pillar can cause enough of an Etheric effect to make a difference?

Should such a spell get traction, I presume the ceremonial magician would want to consider Levi's Ritual of the Four, specifically the bit with the sword cutting a silver cord between the magician and the undesired influence?

And more generally, my reading shows the Conjure practitioner focused on the very nitty gritty of "a problem exists right now - need money, under attack, partner's moving out - and we're Conjuring a solution right here right now." Meanwhile the CGD involves meditation, divination, meditation, daily practice, meditation - basically, building one's self up to have the presence of mind to stay out of the way of life's problems while getting rid of karma to keep destiny from pulling you into problems not of your choosing and building up the magical defenses to take a hit if one becomes unavoidable. I was wondering if you knew of any source of a more contemplative and self-building approach to Conjure/Rootwork/Hoodoo or is it more a matter of different systems are made by different people in different circumstances for fundamentally different purposes?

Or I suppose given the consecration of an amulet in the CGD Druid grade looks a lot like a ceremonial take on a mojo bag, would a contemplative and self-building approach to those traditions look substantially different from the CGD? I suppose I'll have to meditate on that...

replies.title: Re: Rootwork and Ceremonial Magic
replies.content:
1) One of the basic principles of magic is that each plane influences the plane directly beneath it. If you want to work with physical matter, etheric magic is a good way to do it; if you want to work with etheric force, astral magic is well suited to that, and so on. In my experience, regular banishing with any standard ceremonial magic method will keep you safe from the great majority of conjure workings, or anything else that's based on natural magic.

2) What I've been told is that a more contemplative and spiritual approach to conjure is practiced in some African-American magical lodges, but they don't publish their workings. Unless you happen to be black and have a personal recommendation to one of these lodges, you're probably out of luck, as of course am I.

3) I was wondering if anyone would notice that similarity. ;-)

comments.title: a few questions
comments.content:
Hello JMG, I have a few questions burning under my fingernails:

1. Does the amulet you described in your MM FAQ only neutralize the negativity or does it also reflect the negativity back? Please feel free to comment on the mechanism of how it works if you want.

2. Does the protective bath in your MM FAQ hurt the tree that you use in the process (or the tree spirits)?
Please feel free to comment on the mechanism of how it works if you want.

3. Some spiritual teachers talk extremely badly about Tarot use... You yourself said there is a big difference between using Tarot for scrying and for fortune-telling. Could you elaborate on the difference and also on any ethical and non-ethical sides of Tarot use with some examples please?

4. In GD system, both LBRH and Rose Cross Ritual utilize the forces of Tifaret... Is it useless to do them both one after another back-to-back (as one is directing the power towards macrocosmos and another inside, towards the subtle bodies so the does the second one done cancel the effects of the first one?)?

Thanks
JPC

replies.title: Re: a few questions
replies.content:
1) I don't teach mirroring methods, because these days the other guy probably has a mirroring spell in place too, and that means you just get into a tennis match that continues until one side slips -- and that could be you. It discharges the etheric form.

2) No, it's actually good for the tree. Just as trees need the carbon dioxide we breathe out and we need the oxygen they breathe out, expelled etheric force from each is good for the other.

3) Um, if you don't know the difference between scrying and divination I recommend you go back and read any of my introductory books.

4) They don't use the same energies. The R+C ritual uses Tiphareth symbolism but it calls down light from Kether -- that's the Divine White Brilliance or D.W.B. the Golden Dawn papers discuss. The Tiphareth symbolism is intended to bring the practitioner into resonance with his or her own Higher Self, which is in Tiphareth, so he or she can receive the divine benediction down the Path of Gimel from Kether. Thus you don't use it together with the Hexagram ritual because the intentions are different and magic requires unity of will.

comments.title: Esoteric side of the maximum power principle
comments.content:
Following on from your other blogs discussion on Schopenhauer, and the Will, I cycled back to that odd feature of the the ecological/energy theorists - maximum power - and wondered what might be the other side of it. Maximum power on its own doesn't tell a happy story, basically all complex systems compete, and the best way to win the competition, be it short or long term, is to maximise the rate of energy consumption.

It got me cycling back to Charlie Hall's latest publication on the history of maximum power, which, as it turns out, came from Nietsche, who talked about the "will to power". Of course he was talking about Schopenhauer here.

https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/10.1098/rsta.2022.0290

My question is, if there's an esoteric principle behind how our planet, and maybe our solar system is geared to taking energy, which presumably is life force on the material and etheric planes, and using it up. Using it in an irreversible process means entropy, and we don't really know what entropy is.

Could it be that the maximum power principle is just energy 'rounding the buoy', and returning up the planes?

replies.title: Re: Esoteric side of the maximum power principle
replies.content: That might be going on, yes. If the old teachings are correct, though, this is the fifth great cycle of civilization on our planet, and is governed astrologically by Mars, the planet most in keeping with maximum power. It may be that this habit of ours is simply a feature of the present cycle, and the next, Jupiter cycle will be less Nietzschean. It's worth rememebering what ended up happening to Nietzsche...

replies.title: Re: Esoteric side of the maximum power principle
replies.content: So then after the Jupiter cycle we will have the Saturn cycle, the Uranus cycle, and the Neptune cycle, and then there are no more cycles of civilization after that?

replies.title: Re: Esoteric side of the maximum power principle
replies.content:
Jupiter and Saturn, according to the traditional lore; there are seven cycles. (Uranus and Neptune are higher octaves of Mercury and Venus respectively.) The civilizations of the Jupiter cycle will be grander and more glorious than any other, more so than even the solar Atlantean civilization, and also more humane than any other; the civilizations of the Saturn cycle will last much longer than any other -- possibly longer than all the others put together -- but they'll also cover a smaller geographical area than those that came before them.

After that, there will be cultures but no civilizations, until the last human souls have finished going through incarnation; then it'll be the turn of a different life-wave and a different intelligent species.

replies.title: Re: Esoteric side of the maximum power principle
replies.content: I can see how Uranus is a higher octave of Mercury - both have a focus on the sciences and technology but Mercury is focused on skilled manual labour while Uranus is more about automation. But in what sense is Neptune a higher octave of Venus?

replies.title: Re: Esoteric side of the maximum power principle
replies.content: That's the claim made in the old occult literature. I'm not sure how it works either.

replies.title: Re: Esoteric side of the maximum power principle
replies.content:
This is the first I've encountered this thought, so take any speculation from me with appropriate seasoning: that being said, what struck me is that Neptune/Rhea and Uranus/Prometheus both seem to embody "wider," "bigger picture" energies that are nonetheless "in tune" with the narrower, more localized influences of Venus and Mercury. Also, Neptune and Uranus both seem more extreme than their more familiar lower octave pairings - Mercury is (sometimes) about transgressing human categories and boundaries, Uranus about transgressing natural/cosmic boundaries (stealing cattle vs stealing fire, for example), and Venus is about people coming together, where Neptune is about people melding together into a mass.

At least, that's what popped into my mind reading this thread, but as I said, this is a new concept for me, and I'm not especially grounded (heh) in astrology yet, so I may be way off base.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Rubeus Double-Whammy!
comments.content:
I’d like to share a rather unusual experience that I recently had while casting some geomancy charts. As I like to cast charts on matters of political elections from time to time, I decided to cast charts about the upcoming US presidential elections. Given the Caligula-level of degeneracy that the political system has devolved into, I decided NOT to cast charts as to whether either Commula or The Orange One would win the election – ‘cuz winning the election may not mean becoming the next president. Nor did I ask whether either Commula or The Orange One would be the next president – ‘cuz who knows if either of them will be alive on January 20, 2025, or even on November 5, 2024, at this point. Instead, I cast two charts: one for whether the next US president would be a Democrat; the other for whether it would be a Republican.

I cast the Democrat chart first. Thoroughly shuffled my deck of regular cards. First four cards formed Rubeus – and stopped dead in my tracks. Hmmm. I thought I was being honest in asking the question. Whatever. So, I put the cards aside for awhile, thinking that maybe the timing is not good – I do this from time to time when I get Rubeus as the first Mother. The following day, I did the cards once again. First four cards: Rubeus! This has never happened to me before – and I have cast thousands of charts. It was like getting a slap on the wrist with a commandment: Thou Shalt Not Ask This Question!

Ok, ok, sorry - let me try the Republican chart. First four cards formed Conjunctio. Whew! So, I cast the full chart. And what I got was rather interesting: Right Witness - Laetitia; Left Witness - Tristitia; Judge – Carcer. First house figure (Rep): Conjunctio. Seventh house figure (Dem) - Via. Combined figure: Carcer (same as Judge). Neither Conjunctio nor Via appear elsewhere in the chart, nor is there any “company” with the next house (2nd and 8th). Tenth house figure (the goal: president): Rubeus! Rubeus also appears in the fifth house, forming a trine with the Reps and weaker sextile with the Dems. Not exactly a fabulous chart. Needless to say, I continue to expect anything and everything under the Sun regarding this whole presidential thing at least until March-April of next year. What a shale-fest! May the gods have mercy on the American people.

Just thought you might get a wry chuckle out of this incident. Any further insights are welcome!

Ron M

comments.title: A Couple of Random Planetary Questions
comments.content:
1. Do you happen to know the source of the traditional Hebrew God-named associated with the planets?

2. Agrippa writes about using the powers of Jupiter and Venus to counter workings of Saturn and Mars, and vice versa. Presumably, the luminaries can also be used to counter one another. But which of the classical planets would you invoke to counter Mercury?

replies.title: Re: A Couple of Random Planetary Questions
replies.content:
1) No, not off hand. I don't know all that much about the early history of Jewish mysticism.

2) Mercury's his own opposite! He's the one planet whose symbol includes all three of the elements of a planetary symbol -- the cross, the circle, and the crescent. He's also the only androgyne planet, and the only convertible planet -- he takes his character from whatever planet or planets he's in aspect with. Thus the best way to balance him is to figure out how he's being problematic, and then do another working with him when he's conjunct a planet that pulls him in the other direction.

That leads me to think that a lot of guides to various sorts of social interaction reflect planetary principles in this way. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
This is another report from the person who solved a chronic case of insomnia by removing artificial lighting from my life.

I asked two weeks back about the possibility that regularly staying up through part of the night might have major effects on the subtle bodies, based on some intriguing evidence and some odd personal experiences. I think I have disproven this hypothesis: I have borrowed a book from the library on how nighttime was viewed in Early Modern Europe, and while it focuses heavily on that time and place, it talks about night in general in premodern society.

It was common in traditions around the world, ranging from Native Americans to Japanese, to the Mysteries of the Ancient Greeks, for serious spiritual practitioners to regularly stay up quite late or to make themselves wake in the middle of the night, in order to do spiritual practices, on a regular basis; and many religions, such as medieval and early modern Christianity, held that prayers done during nighttime were among the most spiritually advantageous. Further, the laity in a number of traditions were encouraged at least on occasion to spend nights in prayers and reflection: among others, the traditional Easter and Christmas Vigils were incredibly important in earlier forms of Christianity.

Alertness during the night, based on these data points, does not seem to have detrimental effects on spirituality, short or long term, and might even have the opposite effect, at least in premodern societies. I think, however, I have found a plausible mechanism linking the detrimental effects on spirituality to artificial light itself, instead of staying awake during the nights, as well as explaining the evidence from the experiences of these religious traditions. One of the many mechanisms our bodies use to regulate sleep and wakefulness is melatonin, which is secreted from the pineal gland.

It turns out that exposure to light of any intensity reduces the activity of the pineal gland; during the day, it is almost, if not entirely completely shut down. Most electric lights, the gas lights that caught on in urban areas during the 19th century, and most likely, although I have not been able to find enough details to verify this last point yet, the lanterns used by the aristocrats from the 16th century onward who inverted their sleep schedules, are bright enough to make the pineal gland behave as if it is daytime.

Assuming something similar happens on the inner planes, given just how powerful the third eye centre is for spiritual practices, this could easily cause a massive set of problems for spiritually inclined individuals, explain why the aristocrats who inverted their sleep cycle fell into atheism, explain why my spiritual practices have gone into overdrive recently, and why so many traditions made use of various nocturnal practices.

a) Does this hypothesis, which explicitly links subtle plane activity to physical plane biological activity, seem plausible to you?

b) Do you know any good sources on the subtle plane biology (metabiology?) of the pineal gland, beyond what I could find in good general sources on the anatomy of our subtle bodies?

c) I’d like to apologize for having only just seen it, but in response to Princess Cutekitten’s question from two weeks ago, “Do you still use candles? Lanterns? Also, do streetlights and car lights shining in have any effect?”

I actually do not use any artificial light at all during the night right now, aside from the incense stick (which is surprisingly bright if it is in a completely dark room)/candle I light at sunset for evening prayers. I think I will need to change this for winter, but for now this seems to work well enough for me.

As for other lights, I am currently finding that the relatively modest amount of artificial light I from outside that gets through my curtains does not seem to be enough to do much of anything; thankfully, I do not live in a very dense community, I am not on a busy street, and my bedroom faces away from the road, so I have less of a problem than someone in an environment with worse light pollution would face.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, and I can add another data point to it. There's a consistent difference between religious traditions that have practitioners shave the top of their heads, allowing more light to reach the pineal gland (the skull and brain are both transparent to red light), and traditions that have practitioners grow their hair long and cover the top of their heads. The former are always into celibacy and the suppression of emotions; the latter tend to be into active sexuality and the expression of emotions. Thus the influence of the pineal gland on consciousness shows up in another religious context.

2) There's been an enormous amount of writing on the subject but most of it is of very uneven quality -- I'm still looking for useful sources. I've considered a book on the subject one of these days.

comments.title: Apollo in the Holler
comments.content:
I had an interesting dream on Saturday morning. Since you are getting into dream journaling, I thought I would share this one and see if you or anyone else wants to take a crack at it.

My wife and I are driving from one neighborhood to another, and the road gets very rocky. It also goes from urban to rural in a short amount of time. There is a creek on the left side of me, and I am careful not to go into it on the windy, windy road. There are lots of rocks on the road, and at one point, around a sharp turn, I pull over, after just passing under a bridge, and park abruptly on the side. Up ahead is a group of buildings on a flat part. It is a small farm area, or compound anyway, with a double wide, small barns or animal buildings, and several others. A hillbilly in a white wife beater comes over to help us. He is very friendly.

He wants to show us underneath the bridge we have passed under, that connects the ridges above. On one side of the bridge, just past the concrete supports is an entrance to a cave going deep into the hill.

Then we are on his land, and we find ourselves in a small building with a bunch of aunties. A reverend comes in. He is wearing the collar of a priest. He introduces himself. This is some kind of prayer meeting I think. I shake his hand as does my wife. The aunties are kind of hysterical about him, but not in a bad way.

Then my wife goes with the hillbilly to help him feed the pigs. I take a look around. I see these bridges connecting all of these different buildings together. I then also see just how old the place really is. It's in some sense, like an Italian villa, only ramshackle. I turn around and then see a new area that is ruins from long ago. It is a statue of Hermes, but then I am looking at a text. It is the Orphic Hymn to Apollo and I know I have to read this out loud. The words move in the book I am reading from, but I am able to recite the hymn.

This place now seems even much more ancient than I at first thought. Eventually the hillbilly helps us get our car back on the road.

At that point I become somewhat lucid in the dream and I start reading the hymn again, as it seems to be a part of the work I need to do to become a preacher.

From there I fell back into a deep sleep again... and had another wild dream. I won't recount it in full, but it featured a magical sword inscribed with the word ATOR, which I knew on waking up, to be part of the magic square, TORA, TARO, ROTA, etc.

I won't put my own interpretation here, at least not yet, because I'd like to see what people think in the comments first. Thanks, as ever.

One related question: how often should an invocation or hymn be recited to a deity? I always seem to get negative answers in divination, say regarding if I was to recite this hymn once a week until the new year. I was told no. But I still feel like I should begin some work with Apollo. Just not sure how frequently.

replies.title: Re: Apollo in the Holler
replies.content: Good heavens. That's a wild dream! The methods of dream interpretation I'm studying -- my textbook is Strephon Kaplan Williams's The Jungian-Senoi Dreamwork Manual, a classic text from the 1970s -- are intensely personal, and involve various kinds of dialogue between the dreamer and the dream; thus I won't attempt to offer an interpretation. I'll just suggest that written dialogues between yourself and the characters in the dream, letting the first thing that comes into your mind serve for their answers, might be worth your while.

comments.title: Malefic encounter
comments.content:
Hello, JMG.

As always, thank you for providing this forum and for teaching us about the Unseen.

I am writing to ask for insights and guidance regarding an experience I had a few days ago. Apologies if it runs a little long.

I had noticed an esoteric shop that I had never seen before. The one I've been using for several years recently closed and I decided to check out the newly-discovered one. When I first entered, it seemed harmless enough. It soon became apparent that it had a left-hand path orientation.
I looked around carefully, purchased a few things, and proceeded next door to their bookshop. The left-hand orientation was much stronger. Patrons and the man at the cash register were discussing Crowley, and when the proprietor saw me he did a double-take. His hair was dyed brown-blonde and swept back, and he was dressed in a black or very dark blue robe without a cowl. We locked eyes for a moment and he seemed alarmed. It seemed that he would have said something if he wasn't involved in the conversation about Crowley.

I kept moving and began browsing the contents of the shop. All in all, it was the largest and best-organized occult book collection I have yet encountered. It was merchandised to emphasize left-hand offerings, including vintage books about Crowley and Thelema in glass cases. I checked around carefully in case they had older titles I've been looking for, but did not see anything I couldn't live without. I could see and sense that the robed man, though engaged with the initial patrons plus others, kept looking at me. I left without engaging or purchasing anything.

In addition to selling things, the business also hosts "classes" and talks on occult subjects. The attendant in the first part of the shop handed me a list of offerings for this month. More than half had to do with S*tanism or left-hand topics.

Though I felt my practices and working protected me while at the shop, I felt unwell after leaving. I drove away onto a side street and said a prayer. I proceeded to a place where I used the restroom and splashed water on my forehead. I did my best to shake off the experience and go on with my day.

The next morning, I did the SoP followed by discursive meditation. I decided I would meditate on encounter at the bookshop, and I had a singular and unwelcome experience. When I started to think about the man and what happened, it was as if there was a blank, black wall in my mind. I had stray thoughts, but was not able to begin the exploration of my chosen topic. Nothing would happen--no thoughts, insights or flights of fancy would come. This has never happened before, and I am interested in your guidance and insights.

After the attempt at meditation, I performed a Tarot reading. I asked, "What do I need to understand about the consequences of my encounter with the robed man at the __________ book shop?" I did a three-card reading in the manner that you teach on this DW journal. The card for "me" was the Page of Pentacles upright, the situation was The Chariot upright, and the outcome was the reversed VII of Swords. I have a sense of what it means to me, but I am interested in any input you might offer as to interpretation.

I have become more uneasy about what happened. I threw away a the few purchases I had made, along with the listing of "classes". I will, of course, never go there again. Do you have any suggestions about actions I should take to cleanse and protect myself, and long-term effects of the encounter?

Thank you very much,
sun_lion

replies.title: Re: Malefic encounter
replies.content:
1) The bookshop owner probably has protective workings in place, and that's the barrier you sensed. Generally speaking, how good is your clairvoyance?

2) the 7 of swords reversed is the key here; it means that you're making the situation out to be worse than it is. Be like the Charioteer, ride on past, and don't worry about it. The guy at the store probably noticed the energy you've established around your aura by regular practice of the SoP; practitioners of nasty magic tend to find that uncomfortable.

3) You might do a protective bath of the kind described in the Magic Monday FAQ, to clear away any remaining energetic connections, and then do some journaling to help yourself understand what you experienced and why it shook you as much as it did.

replies.content:

2) & 3) I will take your advice.

Sincerely,
sun_lion

comments.title: Polytheistic GD Questions
comments.content:
1: I've recently begun to work with the Polytheistic Golden Dawn material you've posted here, invoking the Hellenic pantheon. When deciding which names to call upon during the Middle Pillar, I was planning on a fairly standard list of the major Olympian deities, but was still split between a few options and decided to take a divinitory approach to settling the issue, involving invocations and prayers and then drawing a few cards for each of two or three names that seemed like good matches, with one extra card drawn for "try again with a new list of names," and going with the most favorable option.

From top to bottom, I ended up with the following: Aphrodite, Athena, Isis, Adonis, Demeter. There were a couple big surprises there, such as the inclusion of Isis meaning that apparently I'm switching to a Hellenistic pantheon instead of a Hellenic one, and the placement of Aphrodite (who happens to be the being in the Patron spot for the other rituals), but overall I feel like the oracles made sense and I'm fairly happy with the result. The only thing that gives me pause is Adonis in the life-force center position, due to the "dying young" bit of his story. The situation doesn't seem so dire in the source materials--the Orphic hymn dedicated to him seems to pretty clearly cast him as a regenerating vegetation god, referring to him as "ever flourishing and bright; At stated periods doom'd to set and rise," and elsewhere in the Orphic hymns he's referred to as, "fair Adonis, never doom'd to die," but I wanted to see if you thought there was any red flag to including a being largely famous for dying in that position.

2: Does your chapter in Llewellyn's Complete Book of Ceremonial Magick include anything going beyond what you've posted here for a Polytheistic Golden Dawn approach? I already have the CGD and CoP if that's relevant.

Thank you very much!
Heliotrope Lily-livered Gorgon

replies.title: Re: Polytheistic GD Questions
replies.content:
1) Not only is that Hellenistic, it's specifically the kind of thing you'd see in theurgic Neoplatonist circles of the kind that took the Mystery initiations very seriously -- thus a good sign. Adonis fits very well in the life force center; you don't mention your gender but in the male, at least, there's a very straightforward biological reality to a god who falls limp to the ground as though dead, and then rises again! No, I don't mean this as a joke; from the point of view of the old Pagans, sexuality in all its robust physical solidity is an expression of the divine.

2) No, if you have the stuff I've posted here you've got the core of the material I put in that chapter. Once it's been in print long enough for a certain contract clause to lapse -- or if it goes out of print before then -- I may do an entire book on the subject, but that's for the future.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,


There is a huge amount of people these days offering training in magic. The types of magic they practice range from grimoire magic to chaos magic and everything in between. Many of these individuals make big claims about their abilities.

It can be hard to decide who to trust.


Q1. In your opinion, what makes a successful magician?


Thanks in advance for your help.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Do you mean by that "how does one become a successful magician?" or "how can I recognize a good magical teacher?" I assume the latter; correct me if I'm wrong.

The signs I'd look for in choosing a teacher or a system are these.

1) Does the teacher have the life they want? Problems and tragedies come into everyone's life, but if the teacher is constantly going on about being ill, or being poor, or being persecuted, or being ignored, or what have you, it's worth asking why they haven't fixed those problems if their magic has any power to it.

2) More generally, how do the promises measure up to the results? If the teacher says that you can become filthy rich, famous, and a babe magnet, they'd better be able to demonstrate this, and so should their senior students.

3) Then again, what are the senior students like, and do you want to become like that? If you're not impressed by them, stay away -- and if the teacher has no senior students at all, or does his or her best to keep students from meeting each other, that's a major red flag.

4) To get down to specifics, does the teacher teach students effective means of banishing? Banishing is as important in magic as washing your hands after using the bathroom is in basic sanitation, and a teacher who won't or can't teach you how to shut down unwanted manifestations is a positive danger to you, their other students, and themselves.

5) And of course, how's their ethics? A teacher who says it's okay to rip people off will probably rip you off, you know, and a teacher who says it's okay to hurt people will probably hurt you. Act accordingly.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There aren't many, for a melancholy reason. You can teach a lot of things by handing somebody a packet of papers and saying, "Study these and I'll make contact with you again in a month," but you can't teach most exercises that way -- the Five Rites are among the few exceptions. Since for 1500 years or so the Western esoteric traditions had to be passed on that way to avoid those annoying visits by the Inquisition, most things that required extended personal instruction had to be dumped.

One place you might look are the writings of Yogi Ramacharaka. Despite the faux-Hindu name, that's our old friend William Walker Atkinson, and his teachings are as Indian as he was; they're classic Western esotericism in Eastern drag. (That was a very common gimmick back in the day.) His books >Hatha Yoga and The Science of Breath are useful introductions to Western exercise and breathwork.

comments.title: Magical ethics of self development
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I have for a long time had a notion of using magic to assist me with uncovering and developing my skills and potential as a writer. I believe personally and was also told that I showed promise as a teenager, but something went amiss and without going into detail decades later the idea is still un realised.

Now I've been practicing the basic DMH practices diligently in recent months and am having some success with the simple element workings (I'm not yet up to all 7 gates in SOP as adding them very slowly).

My issue: despite a growing confidence about using magic generally, when it comes to writing I have trouble shaking the feeling that doing magic to become more creative, a better writer etc would somehow be cheating, making any demonstrated talent less authentic than someone who made it without.

Would the riposte to this idea, from a magic ethics POV, therefore be something like "any magical help is merely giving a push to ability that was always there in potential, but blocked. Magic can't make you a better writer/artist than u could reasonably ever have been any more than it can make anything physically impossible happen"?

Thanks

Posting anonymously to guard my fragile little ego with respect to this topic.

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content:
If it's cheating to use magic to unblock one's talent and become a better writer, then I'm guilty as sin on all counts. The only reason I'm a successful published writer today is that I've used magic relentlessly to achieve that goal. I'm not the only one, either -- not by a long shot. You might be amazed to know how many classic science fiction authors, just for starters, dabbled (or more than dabbled) in magic.

Let's take a deeper look, though. What is magic? The art and science of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will. What is writing? A form of magic. Sit down with a good novel and your consciousness is going to change in accordance with the writer's will. There you are, scrambling through the Mines of Moria alongside the Fellowship of the Ring, your heart pounding as the orcs get closer, even though you've read it before and you know what's going to happen. There's a reason why the word "spell" means both "write a word letter by letter" and "a magic charm"...

As a writer, even a novice who's not yet as good at it as you want to be, you're already practicing magic. You're already trying to learn how to change the consciousness of your readers in accordance with your will. You're already using arcane symbols -- that's what letters and written words are, you know, and it's also what characters, settings, and plot events are. So what you're asking me is, "Is there something wrong with doing all these things in a slightly different way to affect myself, so that I can get better at casting a spell on readers?" Why, no, there isn't. Is there anything wrong with casting that spell on the readers, btw? Not a chance; they're paying good money to enchanters who can weave that kind of spell around them, and if you can provide them with the same service they long for, they'll pay you, thank you, and praise you for it.

The one caution I'll offer is this: always direct your magic for this purpose onto yourself. Focus on enhancing your creative imagination, giving yourself good work habits, and finishing what you start. Don't try to use magic to make people like your work; that always results in blowback -- readers will like your stuff for a short time, and then snap to the opposite extreme and dislike it. You may be able to think of authors who made it big and then suddenly had their careers crash; I'm fairly sure they made this mistake.

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content:
A fascinating reply, thank you! I particularly like the idea of getting better at casting spells on readers that they've volunteered to have cast. And yes, my intention was always to work on myself.

The only thing I'd say is I wish I were at the novice stage: it's more that I hardly write at all, have a very hard and discouraging time when I do, but the feeling I should be doing it never goes away. So my first workings are going to be squarely aimed at getting me writing full stories, even if they are rubbish. I've had parallel issues (not to get into a tedious & frankly miserably neurotic internal saga) for years with my ability to read with enjoyment being spoiled too, which further impacted writing confidence (i.e feelings of "how the heck can I kid myself I could still be a writer when I can hardly read sometimes!") , but improving this latter has already been one of my DMH successes. Which has given me heart to reach further.

I would like to know which Sci fi authors you mean. I'm reading more Ursula le Guin and enjoying even more than usual (thanks to DMH); she is a spell casting writer for me.

Thanks again for encouragement, and in advance for any reply as I'm off to bed now!

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content:
1) I'm going to offer you one of my standard tips for frustrated writers: don't read what you're writing while you're writing it. Most people come out of high school English classes with their ability to write crippled, because they've been taught to edit as they write -- and that's a guaranteed recipe for writer's block. The problem's neurological: the part of your brain that handles creativity and the part that handles assessment interfere with, and when you try to use them both at the same time, your brain freezes up.

What you need to do is write without paying attention to what you're writing. I mean this quite literally. If you have to tape a piece of paper across the screen, do it. Ignore spelling errors, ignore jumbled sentences, ignore everything -- you can fix it later. Just write. Do this for fifteen minutes a day without letting your fingers stop; if all you can think of to write is "I don't know what to write, I don't know what to say, what am I supposed to write," and so on, write that. If you do this every day for a month you'll break through the evil spell cast on you during your schooling (I mean this quite literally, btw) and free up your writing ability.

2) Robert Heinlein, Roger Zelazny, and Fritz Leiber are three of the most well known. I've got my suspicions about André Norton, but she seems to have been very careful to keep her private life private.

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content:
I think part of the problem is that with computers and text editors or document editors, it is now really easy for people to go back to earlier in the document and change what they typed.

When people were still using typewriters, the text gets physically placed on a piece of paper and once that happens it is no longer possible to change what they typed on that piece of paper.

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content: That's certainly made the problem worse, but writer's block has been a reality since long before word processors -- and the advice I've just passed on dates from before then, too. (I originally got it from a science fiction editor at a convention in the 1970s, when computers were still the size of warehouses.)

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content:
This. Exactly this. Write and don’t read it or edit until you are done!

I write magazine features, not books, but the process is the same and JMG nails it. My personal twist is to write with a pen on paper. It makes it much harder to go back and tweak it. Especially when writing titles, ledes or chapter headings, just write write wrie, anything that comes into your head. Don’t edit until the next day when you’ve slept on it, at the very soonest.

—Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content: That's what I did when I broke myself of the habit of writer's block -- I had a notebook and a pen, and my rule was that the pen must not stop moving for 15 minutes straight. I prefer to type into a file these days, but the principle is the same.

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content:
As our host already said, Heinlein and especially Leiber were knowledgeable about magic, and each was married to a woman who was deeply interested in magic and regularly practiced it herself (Heinlein's second wife of his three; Leiber's first of his two). I don't know for certain whether either man ever practiced magic himself, but it seems likely that he might have done some magic together with his wife of the moment.

Marion Zimmer Bradley was perhaps the most knowledgeable occultist and magican among all the major SF&F authors or her era. She ran a regular column on occult topics in The East Village Other in New York in the later 1960s, under the pseudonym Elfrida Rivers.

Shirley Jackson might not be counted among the Science Fiction & Fantasy writers of her era by some (though I would do so), but she certainly practiced practical magic regularly, and made no bones about it. Her magic was basically New-Thought magic, like that of William Walker Atkinsson and many others down to Norman Vincent Peale. (She was raised as a Christian Scientist.)

As for Lovecraft himself, he quite likely experimented with magic in his adolescent years, purely to see -- as a budding scientist -- whether he could make it work himself, but he probably found that he could not. His family had actually owned an antique copy of Cotton Mather's book of miracles and magic in early New England, </>Magnalia Christi Americana, which he seems to have devoured at an early age and cherished all his life.

Ursula LeGuin was the daughter of two eminent California anthropologists, and as such would have been roughly as knowledgeable as her parents about the magic of the Indigenous peoples they studied.

More generally, California has been awash in many forms of magic ever since the 1860s, and almost any SciFi writer who grew up there and then probably had at least a slight famliarity with one or another of its many, many forms.

And I certainly share our host's suspicions about Andre Norton, who at times seems quite sophisticated in her understanding of magic.

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content: Thanks for this. I haven't seen documentation of Zelazny's involvement, but his description of "walking the Pattern" in the first of the Amber novels is so completely reminiscent of the experience of going through the Golden Dawn Neophyte degree that I'm quite sure he must have had that in his past. (Sara and I noticed that independently of each other, and were both intrigued that the other had drawn the same conclusion.)

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics of self development
replies.content:
Take this for what you will, but my own efforts at writing became much more unblocked with regular practice of the SOP which will help hopefully also help clear out some of the inner gunk that is hindering you as you become elementally more balanced.

More generally, I've taken much of our hosts writing advice to heart and got a book in print at age 44, achieving a childhood dream. It's never too late, and many commercially successful authors got their start in middle age (Clive Cussler comes to mind).

There is a specific section in the DMH where it talks about doing a working for a stone to enhance the creativity and skill of a musician. The same thing can be done for writing.

The use of affirmations, as taught in the Way of the Golden Section, can also be tailored to your own goals and aims.

Wishing you luck, and now to open up a .doc file and unplug the internet for awhile.

Justin Patrick Moore

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A while back, I read through Christian Gilson's A Path to the Grail, which presents some of the Guild of the Master Jesus material, and provides extensive commentary. (There's an interview with Gilson at https://www.holythornpress.com/christiangilsoninterview .)

The book is quite interesting for several reasons, including almost-rapprochement with Crowley's work that is typical of the Dion Fortune tradition. Gilson's commentaries aren't simply elucidations, but also some critical engagements with Fortune's ideas and procedures. It is not a complete archive of the Guild material, but rather a presentation of Fortune's articles about it, the Guild Mass, and some associated material (e.g., her articles on the Winged Bull). It also contains a weird stylistic habit that seems unfortunately common among people who write about Fortune -- referring to her as "Dion" (while almost every other author is referred to initially by personal and family name, and afterward by family name).

However, there is one point that intrigued me, and prompted a question. In discussing the building of mental images during the Mass, Fortune remarks (p 163) that this does assuming the image of Jesus a a god-form, "for we hold that the power invoked by His name is too purely spiritual to find a channel through any human consciousness, however highly trained." Immediately afterward, she makes a reference to the Catholic priesthood, only to deflect away to a discussion of the priesthood of all believers.

In his commentary, Gilson (p 171-2) adds that in magical work with god-forms, the image is one specialized aspect of the Divine, whereas the Christian conception is that Jesus is a direct incarnation of Deity Itself, "both fully human and fully God", and that, "No initiate is ready for such an experience, the result would be challenging even for the strongest ego."

However, it seems to me that one of the core claims of at least the Roman Catholic church is that, in the Mass, and some other sacraments, the priest is not acting in his own person: the sacramental actions are done by Christ, who by ordination in some sense indwells the priest. Now, a full realization of this connection could indeed be a burden, but it seems to be part of the bundle.

Again, it seems to me that a contemplative priest, say, a member of a contemplative order, could do worse than to assume the appropriate Deific form at the consecration in the Mass, or in hearing confessions, for example. And though it might seem paradoxical, putting on and putting off the Deific form could itself serve as a protection for ordinary consciousness, rather than an overloading, or an occasion for inflation.

So, my question is, what do you think? Is this reluctance on the part of Fortune and Gilson fully justified? Are they simply being respectful of a tradition to which they do not have full initiatory access? Would such use of God-forms be reasonable for priests ordained in the appropriate tradition(s), but not for others? It's actually a big question, or cluster of questions. Any comments would be welcome.

Thanks!

Smaragdine Senex

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I also have a copy of the book, though I haven't had the chance to read it yet -- I've been up to my eyeballs in WB Yeats's A Vision and related literature, among other things. As for your question, it makes no sense to me to insist that the godform of Jesus shouldn't be an option for a Christian celebrant. It's central to mainstream Christian theology that Jesus was wholly human as well as wholly divine, so from within that theology, it's not true at all that the Christ current "is too purely spiritual to find a channel through any human consciousness" -- it was channeled through a wholly human (as well as wholly divine) consciousness, that of Jesus of Nazareth.

I suspect that what's going on here is a very English habit of trying not to trespass on the prerogatives of the Established Church. Anglican theology holds that a priest performing the sacraments acts as the instrumentality of Christ, so Fortune and Gilson both seem to have backed away from any such claim. Interestingly, groups here in the US that took up the Guild of the Master Jesus work generally made a point of getting apostolic succession and taking on the full responsibility of the sacramental work, which the Guild in England avoided doing.

replies.title: The Guild
replies.content:
Thank you for the response. Yes, I did think that there would be a justification for doing so based on incarnational theology -- especially with respect to Eucharistic matters.

It may also well be that they were both avoiding trespassing on the prerogatives of the Established Church -- though Gilson is much more overt in his criticisms of that church (and the weakening of its sacramental power) than Fortune allowed herself to be.

Thanks for the data point about people pursuing the Guild work in the US. You have a much broader (and deeper) range of contacts with the occult and esoteric world than I do.

(The only personal connection I have with Guild work was through a person who obtained episcopal consecration for himself and a couple of his followers; he turned to the Guild liturgy in part because he worked in a tradition derived from DNF, and in part because it seemed to him less encumbered with dubious egregoric connections than some of the other liturgies available to him.)

Smaragdine Senex

Less because it says anything much more about the subject than our host just mentioned than because it has some references to less-familiar-to-westerners examples, but I thought I'd mention The Deities Are Many by Jordan Paper, which I just read on the recommendation of a few other commenters here. Mostly it's about how to understand the nature of divinity from within polytheistic contexts (mostly North American indigenous and Chinese in the case of the author), but in discussing the ways that deities interact with humans in polytheistic traditions, he talks about the apparently huge role that "spirit writing" plays in traditional Chinese religion - it's quite common for mediums to channel direct communication from a God, write it down as it comes, and for that writing to be treated as the the God speaking for him/herself. The "Further Reading" chapters include some translations into English of a handful of the apparently voluminous body of works.

Hope this helps, but whether you end up using it or not, good luck in your work!
Jeff

replies.title: Runes
replies.content: If you like the Runes, might I suggest a small detour?  Go look at the hexagrams of the I-Ching and cross-compare. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not sure if this speaks to your specific experience of familiarity, but on your last question, the Elder Futhark is by far the most popular set of Runestaves for magic and divination in the modern Runic revival (kickstarted mostly by Edred Thorsson in the 80s, though he was heavily inspired by the Romantic revival of Guido von List with his Armanen Runes), and for the most part, they're only used for magic and divination, so it might be that there's a good, clear egregore there that you're brushing up against. As for a "long history in occultism," the short answer is "yeah, pretty much." The longer answer is there's debate about whether the Runes were used for magical and other occult purposes from the very beginning, but most of the arguments against strike me as materialists putting their fingers in their ears and going "I'm not listening!" (that's likely slightly unfair, but I think the arguments are very weak, and the case for magical use of Runes seems overwhelming). Through the early modern period, various Nordic occultists made more or less use of Runes, and much of the Icelandic grimoire tradition includes Runes and pseudo-Runes as arcane symbols. Then you get into the romantic and modern revivals I already mentioned.

Of course, from within Heathen cosmology, there's also the fact that the Runestaves are thought to literally be representations of the working of consciousness, as won for humanity by the God of (among other things) consciousness, so there's always that too.

Whatever the reason for the resonance you felt, should you feel called to investigate further, I can tell you that there is a lot of worth to be found in meditating on the individual staves, their meaning, order, placements within the overall sequence ("opposites" (1 and 24, 2 and 23, etc), pairs (1 & 2, 3 & 4, etc)), position in each aett, the three main Rune Poems (Anglo-Saxon, Norwegian, and Icelandic), and more. If you're interested in reading about all this, this list from Norse Mythology for Smart People gives a pretty good spread of views on them: https://norse-mythology.org/runes/the-best-books-on-the-runes/

A few addendums for that list from me, though: 1) Thorsson's three books on that list (under that name, anyway, not counting the one under his real name, Stephen Flowers) would best be replaced by his newer Big Book of Runes and Rune Magic, which collects the contents of those three books with a few changes and additions. 2) Speaking of Thorsson, his book Alu is a helpful follow-up to the material in point 1, if you find his approach congenial. 3) The list is a bit weak on the mundane/historical development of the Runes and the evidence for it, for which I'd recommend Stephen Pollington's Runes: Literacy in the Germanic Iron Age. 4) If you are mostly interested in the Runes for meditation fodder, Stanzas of the Old English Rune Poem is an incredibly in-depth translation, commentary, and esoteric exegesis of the "Anglo-Saxon Rune Poem," which has the benefit of covering all of the Runes of the Elder Futhark plus a few more. It can be found free online here: https://runicwisdom.info/StnzOerp_Stanfield_30May2012.pdf

All of that may be way more than you want or need right now, but thought I'd err on the side of sharing things that might be interesting or helpful. It's a bit late in the day for much discussion here, but if you have any follow-ups, feel free to drop me a private message here on dreamwidth or an email via my website.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.content:
Hello John Michael,

A while back on the other blog you had discussed that the best way to push forward your magical vision is to focus on your vision and ignore what your enemy is doing (“A Primer of Magical Combat”).

What should one do if someone else who is working in opposition to you joins a community that you’re a part of?

I believe this is relevant because my vision has a community aspect to it (but this particular community I am a part of is not one that I created or lead).

In my case I don’t believe the people I am thinking of are intentionally trying to oppose my vision, but they are definitely working against it by their actions (whether intentional or not).

Should I try to influence them to leave? Or change their mind and leave on their own accord? Should I try to visualize them changing their ways and joining my vision? Or should I simply try to avoid them and ignore anything they do that might interfere with my vision?

replies.title: Re: Magical combat?
replies.content: Once you make the mistake of getting into a magical fight, you simply reinforce what you oppose. Focus all your energy on building your vision, and pay no attention to their opposition at all. That's the tai chi approach -- if the other guy throws a punch, you just move deftly out of the way, so their fist goes flying uselessly through empty air.

replies.title: Re: Magical combat?
replies.content: Thank you.

comments.title: Mundane astrology questions and one on Magic
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Thank you again for hosting this space. I had two questions related to mundane astrology that have come up while reading through some posts here, specifically the Thema Mundi prediction and analysis.

1) My first question is what a mixed triplicity means? I quote from your Thema Mundi post:

"Ruling Planet: Scorpio rises in this chart, and Mars is therefore its ruler. Mars is very mildly dignified in Virgo, the sign of his mixed triplicity..."

I wasn't sure what triplistic relationship Mars bore to Virgo because I was under the impression the triplicities were synonymous with the elements, for example the fire signs Aries, Leo, Sagittarius forming a triplicity or the earth signs Taurus, Virgo, Capricorn forming another. Virgo is in a triplistic relationship to Capricorn, the sign of Mars' exaltation but not any of the signs associated with Mars. Just wanted clarification on how Virgo is the sign of Mars' mixed triplicity and what a mixed triplicity means generally in astrology.

2) My second question is, all other factors being ignored (which I understand is impossible), what effect Saturn has when it is in opposition to the Sun on a solar return in mundane astrology? I would also ask what effect this has in natal astrology (though I understand not your specialty) given I have just had a solar return and Saturn is stationed exactly in opposition to my natal Sun and will be for a the next few months.

I understand if you can't give very specific answers without knowing the houses and other planets but if there are some methods to harmoniously work with or meet the oppositional influence of Saturn until he passes through his opposition to my natal Sun, I would be interested to hear them. I have been practicing GD Magic for about a year but have not advanced very far in the LRM course, still only doing Meditation, QC, LBRP, Middle Pillar, so I suspect magical solutions probably not very effective in my case, not yet at least.

3) This brings me to a third question: Is the following an appropriate sequence for magical practice?:

-Opening
-Relaxation exercise
-QC
-LBRP
-QC
-Middle Pillar
-QC
-Discursive Meditation
-Closing

I'm in the ninth lesson of the LRM course, having advanced rather slowly and wanted to clarify whether the sequence was appropriate and effective based on someone mentioning the benefit of banishing before discursive meditation.

Best Regards,

Egyptian Vulture

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology questions and one on Magic
replies.content:
1) I've never seen a good explanation of the Dorothean mixed triplicities, but they appear to work.

2) As with any other such question, I can't interpret that one aspect unless I know the rest of the chart. Sun opposite Saturn means one thing, for example, when the two planets are in the second and eighth house respectively, the Sun is afflicted by square with Mars, and Saturn is in his exaltation, and something completely different when the planets are in the 11th and 5th house respectively, the Sun is trine Jupiter, and Saturn is peregrine!

3) That's fairly close to the way I did it when I was field testing the practices in that book, so you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology questions and one on Magic
replies.content: Thanks for the response. I'll have to do some research on Dorotheus's mixed triplicities and their applications.

replies.title: Re: Freight yards full…
replies.content: Since the use or non-use of that word is purely a social habit and depends on who you hang out with, I don't propose to judge! More generally, hat's fascinating, and not surprising. It's quite common for people who were really good at something in one life to pick it up quickly and easily in the next. Mozart is the obvious example -- what else can explain composing music and playing on the concert stage at the age of five? -- but there are many others.

comments.title: The deva kingdom
comments.content: Did anyone get around to doing the work which fortuna suggested would be ‘interesting’ on ‘deranged’ people who may have had a deva phase in their karmic record? Do you have any thoughts about where she’s right on in the chapter on non-human pathologies and where you might differ? I experience myself much like the non-person she describes, on some layer let’s say. My whole childhood I had a recurring nightmare of being alone on a sea that was cartoonishly turbulent rising and falling so that it was actually a falling dream but in water. Sometimes a ship way off in the distance. Sometimes coming in like a tsunami crest into land and then terrifying heights like falling from clouds. As I got mastery over my stranded social situation, sexual connection (which has phases of intensity something like how she describes the trip to the mountain wind in this chapter), and self-preservation through becoming very high functioning academically and in some other ways, well the relationship to water in my dreams changed. Falling went away and once I did glide over water. I always pay extra attention to any water dreams because they signify something important. I certainly can relate to being both weirdly magnetic in spite of like beard, average face, unassuming clothes, good curvy body. I can channel a primal howl as well as anyone if we ever seen, not like impersonating a wolf, though I have as close as I can get to those for housemates, but like ‘civilization-scale grief for what is lost and desecrated of nature’ or ‘bacchanal party’ . But I’m also kind of a robot, ‘ordinary human motives/feelings’ do not move them. Like one time a guy I was meeting in Spanish after a few days he said ‘your Spanish is almost *too* good,’ meaning not that I learned too quickly and well but that I learned like a computer a bit and it didn’t sound like natural language. I’ve had the passing feeling of I might accidentally fall off this roof during a time with a lot of access and social time spent on a 3 story building during college where indeed at least one someone had fallen off and died in the past. Like I had great familiarity with a lot of what she said about water and air elementals but took offense to some of it like… it doesn’t mean you can’t feel pity or have a compass just because a,b, and c read true to you. I know she wasn’t trying to talk to people who might sense that they are the bad (partly (not)) human, that she’s trying to protect against their(our?) ‘singular detrimental effect’ on those close. I take a lot of care and I’m certainly not of singular detrimental effect tho I sense that I can have a cataclysmic effect on male partners (who I only choose if they are also already kind of like me, and kind of with an uncertain future, tho I don’t sometimes see if consciously at the outset), and this effect is troubling to me. My work is useful and I’m good at it and I tend to focus there, but anyhow this chapter really hit with me and I wonder if you have thoughts you would share on it, or other experiences, or other resources to read.

replies.title: Re: The deva kingdom
replies.content:
1) I don't know of anybody who's pursued the work that Fortune suggested, no. That's unfortunate, as it's worth doing, but it would take someone with a good background in occultism and psychotherapy, and that combination isn't too common these days.

2) It's quite possible that's what you are. These days, especially, with so many new bodies to fill, there are quite a few nonhumans in human bodies -- I'm fairly sure most come straight from the animal kingdom, without any kind of adequate transition, but elementals and other nonphysicals are doubtless getting in there. As for Fortune's comments, she was overgeneralizing from the few cases she'd studied. I've had certain dealings with elemental spirits in their normal state, and some of them are quite capable of pity -- water elementals in particular tend to be very sensitive to emotions and get deeply upset when they think they've caused distress.
